strict_win_deltas.delta: P1's readJson helper takes distinct error codes per call site so a bad input file yields 'invalid_json' while a bad catalog file yields 'catalog_invalid_json', whereas P2's readJsonFile hardcodes 'invalid_json' for every parse failure regardless of which file caused it, collapsing the error taxonomy.

P1 guards every catalog field before use (`!catalog.products || !hasOwn(...)`, `catalog.line_promotions || []`, `catalog.taxable_codes && ...`), while P2 does `catalog.line_promotions.find(...)` and `catalog.taxable_codes[product.tax_code]` with no existence checks, so a malformed/partial catalog.json throws an uncaught TypeError instead of the required single JSON error object on stderr.

Since the spec requires catalog.json to be the sole source of truth that can change without a code change, P1's defensive checks around catalog.products/tax_rates/coupons/line_promotions/taxable_codes keep the 'exactly one JSON error, no stderr crash' contract intact across catalog variations, while P2's direct property access assumes today's exact catalog shape and would silently break that contract on a catalog edit.

P1's parseInputFlag is a plain `argv.indexOf('--input')` lookup that tolerates any surrounding argv tokens, while P2's parseInputFlag loop contains `if (argv[index] !== '--input' || inputPath !== null) return null;`, which returns null the moment it scans past the --input pair into any other token, a subtle bug that spuriously rejects otherwise-valid invocations.
